original_abstract:
This paper evaluates the technical and formal feasibility of using calcareous fly ash, a byproduct of lignite combustion, as an active mineral component in hydraulic road binders and masonry cements. While current concrete standards limit the use of this material as a Type II additive, its pozzolanic and hydraulic properties offer significant potential for infrastructure applications. The study analyzes calcareous fly ash from the Bełchatów Power Station, testing various binder formulations that incorporate both raw and ground ash, as well as ternary blends containing siliceous fly ash and fluidized bed combustion (FBC) ash.

Experimental results demonstrate that calcareous fly ash is a highly effective constituent, often surpassing the activity indices of traditional siliceous fly ashes. The research indicates that by adjusting the proportions of cement and different ash types, it is possible to produce hydraulic road binders (HRB) in strength classes 5, 12.5, and 22.5 that meet the requirements of European standards and technical approvals. Furthermore, the inclusion of limestone was found to improve the rheological properties and water demand of the mixtures. The findings conclude that mechanical activation through grinding significantly enhances the reactivity of the ash, providing a viable pathway for the mass management of industrial byproducts in road construction and non-structural masonry applications.
humanized_abstract:
Calacreous fly ash as an active mineral component in hydraulic road binders and in masonry cements? The title clearly indicates the subject matter discussed in this paper. Aiming at determining technical and formal conditions for the application of calcareous fly ash as an active mineral component in hydraulic road binders and in masonry cements, the paper deals with the applicability of this byproduct of lignite combustion in concrete. Although the applicable standard (PN-81/R-73014) enables only applying calcareous fly ash as the Type II additive, owing to its pozzolanic and, to a certain extent, hydraulic action it is believed that this material can be also effectively applied in construction materials for infrastructure. The calcareous fly ash from Bełchatów Power Station was used for investigations on the hydraulic road binders of different compositions: the binder containing the untreated and the ground fly ash as well as the ternary composition including the siliceous fly ash and the FBC-ash.

Calcareous fly ash was obtained from an Italian power station where lignite is burned. Its performance as a cementitious component was found to be very high (much higher than that of the usual siliceous fly ashes). By properly adjusting the ratio between the two types of fly ashes and between the fly ashes and the portland cement, it is possible to manufacture hydraulic road binders in classes 5, 12.5 and 22.5 conforming to current European standards for design and technical approval. The inclusion of limestone also yielded significant improvements with regard to flowability and water consumption. The mechanical activation of fly ashes through grinding leads to a sharp increase in reactivity, opening a new perspective for the large-scale utilization of fly ashes in road building and in unstructural masonry.
